name: Warm Springs Reservoir
short_description: BLM public land near Warm Springs Reservoir offers free rockhounding for agate, jasper, and chalcedony. High desert terrain with excellent collecting potential in washes and hillside outcrops.
description:
Warm Springs Reservoir sits in Harney County's high desert basin, offering rockhounds free public access to BLM land with good potential for finding quality agates, jaspers, and other silica-rich minerals. This remote location is ideal for serious collectors willing to explore beyond the most popular Oregon rockhounding sites. The open sagebrush terrain and exposed volcanic geology make it relatively easy to spot specimens in washes and along reservoir terraces.

The area's geology reflects the Basin and Range province, where Miocene-era volcanic activity deposited thick sequences of rhyolite and basalt. Weathering and erosion have concentrated secondary minerals including agate nodules, banded jasper, and occasional obsidian in the alluvial washes and hillside exposures. The high desert environment keeps the landscape relatively open, making surface collecting productive.

Collecting is straightforward here: BLM regulations allow personal use rockhounding with a 25-pound daily limit, no permit required. The terrain is generally accessible, though distances to productive collecting areas can be significant. Most collectors focus on the washes feeding the reservoir and the exposed hillsides to the north and east. Water access at the reservoir makes this a viable multi-hour collecting trip.

Expect solitude and open country. This is not a developed recreation area, so bring plenty of water, sun protection, and a detailed map. The remoteness is part of the appeal - you'll likely have your chosen collecting zone to yourself.
history: Warm Springs Reservoir and the surrounding high desert basins were shaped by Miocene-era volcanism (roughly 25-5 million years ago) that produced the extensive rhyolite and basalt sequences of the Basin and Range province. Subsequent basin-fill deposition and erosional processes concentrated secondary silica minerals - agates, jasper, and chalcedony - into collectable quantities in modern washes and hillside outcrops. This region saw limited commercial mining due to its remote location, but remains a productive source for recreational rockhounders.
tips: Focus on the active and seasonal washes that drain toward the reservoir - these tend to have better concentration of rounded and polished specimens. Look for colorful agate nodules in the tan and reddish gravels, and banded jasper fragments on exposed hillsides where volcanic rocks are weathering. Bring a heavy hammer and chisel if you plan to extract nodules from harder host rock; surface collecting alone may yield scattered pieces. The area receives very little rain, so washes are stable and safe to explore, but always scout terrain ahead for loose rock and be prepared for intense sun reflection off light-colored gravels.
terrain_notes: High desert sagebrush terrain at approximately 4,100-4,500 feet elevation. Rolling hills and bajadas with sparse low brush and scattered juniper. Terrain is generally open and walkable with uneven, rocky footing. Most productive collecting areas are within 0.5-2 miles of the reservoir shoreline, accessible via informal trails and cross-country walking. Washes are shallow and stable. No significant elevation changes, but distances and heat exposure are the primary physical demands.
best_season: April - June, September - October. Late spring and early fall offer moderate temperatures and lower risk of afternoon thunderstorms in the high desert. Summer heat (July-August) can exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit with intense sun exposure; winter (November-March) brings cold mornings and possible snow at higher elevations, though midday collecting is usually feasible.
written_directions: From Burns, take US 395 south for approximately 25 miles toward the town of Fields. Near milepost 74, look for BLM access roads heading east toward Warm Springs Reservoir. The most direct public access is via unsigned dirt roads that branch from US 395; a detailed BLM map of Harney County is essential. Four-wheel drive is not required for main access roads, but high-clearance vehicles are recommended. Allow 45 minutes to 1 hour from Burns to reach productive collecting areas. No developed parking area; park off the main road on stable, non-vegetated ground.
road_conditions: Gravel and dirt roads in variable condition. Some washboard; no major washouts typically, but spring runoff can temporarily damage road surfaces. High-clearance vehicle recommended but not required. Road surface can be sandy in places.
parking_notes: No developed parking area. Park in wide turnouts along access roads or on stable, non-vegetated ground near productive washes. Capacity is unlimited but parking is informal. Ensure vehicle does not block water access or BLM management roads.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or large-scale processing equipment. No collection of protected plants or wildlife. Stay on or near established roads to minimize impact on vegetation.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No protected plants or animals
nearest_services: Burns, Oregon, approximately 40-50 miles northwest. Full services including fuel, food, lodging, and water. Small cafe and general store. No services directly at or near the reservoir.
accessibility_notes: Open high desert terrain with uneven, rocky footing. No wheelchair access beyond parking area. Suitable for hikers with moderate fitness; terrain is not steep but distances and heat exposure are significant. Sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support recommended. No shade available in most areas. Very accessible for most rockhounders; primary challenge is distance and sun exposure rather than technical terrain.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Warm Springs Reservoir in Oregon?
faq.answer: Warm Springs Reservoir is best known for agate, jasper, chalcedony, and occasional obsidian. Agates here appear in nodular form with varied colors including white, tan, red, and banded patterns. Jasper specimens are often found as fragments in washes with distinctive striping and color banding. Most collectors focus on surface finds and weathered material in the alluvial washes.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect rocks at Warm Springs Reservoir BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on this BLM public land. You are allowed to collect for personal use only without advance authorization. However, you must follow BLM collection limits: maximum 25 pounds per day per the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA).

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Warm Springs Reservoir free?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Warm Springs Reservoir is completely free. There are no access fees, permit fees, or daily use charges. This is public BLM land open to recreational collecting at no cost.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Burns, Oregon?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Warm Springs Reservoir is April through June and September through October. These months offer moderate temperatures and lower precipitation, making for comfortable multi-hour collecting trips. Summer (July-August) brings intense heat over 95 degrees Fahrenheit, while winter can bring snow and cold mornings.

faq.question: How difficult is it to find rocks at Warm Springs Reservoir for beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Warm Springs Reservoir is beginner-friendly because the terrain is open and relatively flat, making it easy to spot specimens in washes and hillside exposures. However, the location is remote and requires a 45-minute drive from Burns plus significant distance on foot to reach productive areas. Bring plenty of water, a detailed map, and a hammer. Success depends more on time investment and persistence than technical skill.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding at Warm Springs Reservoir?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are allowed on BLM public land, including Warm Springs Reservoir. Keep your dog under control and be prepared for extreme sun exposure and heat, especially in summer months. There is no shade or water sources along most collecting areas, so bring adequate water for both you and your pet. The remote terrain and wildlife (including potential snakes) mean you should keep your dog close and watch for hazards.

meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony at Warm Springs Reservoir on free BLM public land near Burns, Oregon. No permit required. Beginner-friendly high desert collecting.
